subsection.title: Statement in forms
subsection.p: If S is an oriented surface with oriented boundary \partial S, then \boxed{\int_{\partial S}\omega=\int_S d\omega.}

subsection.title: Classical statement
Hypotheses

md.mrow: 1. \amp S\text{ is an oriented piecewise smooth surface.}
2. \amp \partial S\text{ has the boundary orientation induced by }S.
3. \amp P,Q,R\text{ have continuous first partial derivatives near }S.
4. \amp \omega\text{ is defined on a region containing }S.

subsection.p: Boundary orientation is given by the right-hand rule: \boxed{\text{thumb points along the chosen normal, fingers curl along }\partial S.}

subsection.title: Proof idea
subsection.p: Now \mathbf r^*\omega is a 1-form in the uv-plane.
Apply Green's theorem in the parameter region: \int_{\partial D}\mathbf r^*\omega=\int_D d(\mathbf r^*\omega).
Use the pullback identity: \boxed{d(\mathbf r^*\omega)=\mathbf r^*(d\omega).}

subsection.title: What can fail
subsection.p: If the surface normal is reversed, then the boundary orientation reverses and both sides change sign.
The field must be defined on the surface.
Singularities can break the theorem if they lie on the chosen surface.
If S has no boundary, then \partial S=\varnothing, so Stokes gives \boxed{\int_S d\omega=0.}

subsection.title: Moral
subsection.p: Stokes' theorem is Green's theorem on a curved surface. The pullback flattens the surface into a parameter region where Green's theorem can do the work.
